record_office_terminology.land_records: County Clerk / County Recorder (varies by county; in Cook County, the Recorder of Deeds)
record_office_terminology.probate: Circuit Court (handles probate matters — wills, estates, guardianships, adoptions)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Clerk (issues marriage licenses; county-level records from formation)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1916 (statewide birth/death registration). Cook County (Chicago) birth/death records from 1871 (post-fire). Scattered county records earlier. Some city records (e.g., Peoria, Springfield) pre-date statewide registration.

burned_county_authority: FamilySearch Research Wiki — Illinois Burned Counties; Illinois State Archives county records guides; county-level histories. Cook County is the major 'burned county' due to the 1871 Great Chicago Fire which destroyed the courthouse and most county records.
administrative_notes: Illinois was the 21st state, admitted December 3, 1818 from Illinois Territory (created 1809). Previously part of Northwest Territory (1787), then Indiana Territory (1800). French colonial settlements (Cahokia 1699, Kaskaskia 1703) predate American governance by over a century. The American Bottom (Mississippi River floodplain in St. Clair, Madison, Randolph, Monroe counties) was the first settled area. State capitals: Kaskaskia (1818-1820), Vandalia (1820-1839), Springfield (1839-present). Illinois is a federal land state — all original land patents through U.S. General Land Office. The Military Tract (western IL) was set aside for War of 1812 bounty land. Cook County (Chicago) dominates the state by population. The 1871 Great Chicago Fire destroyed the Cook County courthouse and most pre-1871 county records.

core_repositories.description: Primary state repository for Illinois. Houses county records on microfilm, state government records, military records, land records, vital records indexes, and manuscript collections. The Illinois Public Domain Land Tract Sales Database is available online. Holds Illinois Regional Archives Depository (IRAD) central records.

Premier genealogical research library for the Midwest. Holds local and family histories, city directories, maps, church records, and manuscript collections. The Newberry's genealogy collection includes the Draper Manuscripts (frontier era), extensive Midwest church records, and the Genealogical Index of the Newberry Library (GINL).

core_repositories.repository: Universities across Illinois (Eastern IL, Illinois State, Northern IL, Southern IL-C, Southern IL-E, U of I, U of I-Springfield, Western IL)
core_repositories.description: University-based archives system holding county records including circuit court case files, county board proceedings, tax records, and county commissioners records. Each IRAD location serves a cluster of surrounding counties. Essential for accessing original county records not available at the county level.

core_repositories.source_name: Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library and Museum
core_repositories.description: Holds Illinois newspaper microfilm collection (largest in the state), manuscript collections, Illinois history materials, and genealogical resources. The newspaper collection is invaluable for obituaries, legal notices, and local history across all 102 counties.

core_repositories.source_name: Chicago History Museum (formerly Chicago Historical Society)
core_repositories.description: Major research center for Chicago and Cook County history. Holds manuscript collections, photographs, maps, and records surviving the 1871 fire. Essential for Cook County research, especially pre-1871 period where county records were destroyed.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections: county records (deeds, marriages, probate, court), vital records, cemetery records, military records, and church records. Extensive microfilm partnerships with Illinois counties and Illinois State Archives provide digitized county-level records.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability, land district information, and links to digitized microfilm. Includes Illinois burned counties list and research strategies for each county.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Illinois newspapers from the 1800s forward. Includes major papers (Chicago Tribune, Illinois State Journal, Quincy Whig) and hundreds of smaller county weeklies. Legal notices, obituaries, and estate proceedings are valuable genealogical sources.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library / University of Illinois / various
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Illinois Digital Newspaper Collections (IDNC) provides access to digitized Illinois newspapers not in Chronicling America. Includes many small-town county weeklies documenting legal notices, estates, marriage announcements, and community events across rural Illinois.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: County Circuit Courts / NARA Great Lakes Region (Chicago) / FamilySearch
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Illinois was a major immigration destination, especially Chicago. Pre-1906 naturalizations filed at any court of record — county Circuit Courts, city courts, and federal courts. Post-1906 copies at NARA Great Lakes Region in Chicago. Major immigrant groups: German (largest), Irish, Swedish, Norwegian, Polish, Czech, Italian, Lithuanian, Greek, Jewish, and many other Eastern and Southern European groups.

WPA project that translated and indexed articles from Chicago's ethnic newspapers (1860s-1930s) in 22 languages. Documents immigrant community life, social organizations, and individuals. Languages include German, Polish, Czech, Lithuanian, Swedish, Norwegian, Italian, Greek, and others. Available at the Newberry Library.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Illinois African American and Black Codes Records
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Illinois State Archives / Newberry Library / Vivian G. Harsh Collection (Chicago Public Library)
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Although Illinois was a free state under the Northwest Ordinance, it had a complicated racial history. The 1819-1848 Black Codes required free African Americans to register with county clerks. Indentured servitude of African Americans persisted into the 1840s. Records include: Black Code registrations (1819-1848), Underground Railroad documentation, Freedmen's Aid Society records, Great Migration records (1910-1970), and African American church records. The Vivian G. Harsh Collection at Chicago Public Library is the largest African American research collection in the Midwest.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Chicago Defender and African American Newspapers
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Vivian G. Harsh Collection / ProQuest Historical Newspapers
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: The Chicago Defender (founded 1905) was one of the most influential African American newspapers in the U.S. and was instrumental in promoting the Great Migration. Contains obituaries, social columns, church news, and community events. ProQuest has digitized the Defender. Other African American newspapers include the Chicago Bee, Broad Ax, and various local editions.

military_records.description: Civil War records: Illinois provided approximately 259,000 soldiers to the Union Army — fourth most of any state. Illinois Adjutant General records. War of 1812, Black Hawk War (1832), Mexican War, and WWI/WWII records. Illinois Veterans Home (Quincy) records. The Illinois Civil War Muster and Descriptive Rolls database is searchable online.

The Black Hawk War (1832) involved thousands of Illinois militia members including Abraham Lincoln. Muster rolls, payrolls, and pension records document participants. Many early Illinois settlers served. Records at Illinois State Archives and NARA.

land_and_property.source_name: Illinois Public Domain Land Tract Sales Database
land_and_property.description: Online searchable database of original land purchases from the federal government in Illinois. Covers land office sales from 1814 forward. Includes purchaser name, date, description, acreage, and price. Essential for tracing first landowners in any Illinois county.

land_and_property.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Illinois
land_and_property.description: Federal land patents for Illinois. As a public-land state, all original land entries were through federal land offices. Cash entries, credit sales, military bounty warrants, and preemption patents. The Military Tract in western Illinois is particularly well-documented.

vital_records.source_name: Illinois Department of Public Health — Division of Vital Records
vital_records.repository: Illinois Department of Public Health, Springfield
vital_records.description: State vital records: births and deaths from January 1, 1916 (statewide registration). For earlier events, check county clerk records, church records, cemetery records, and city health department records. Cook County (Chicago) has birth/death records from 1871 (post-fire). Marriage records at county clerk offices from county formation.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Various — Illinois State Archives, Newberry Library, diocesan archives, denominational archives, FamilySearch
church_and_cemetery.description: Church records are essential for Illinois genealogy, especially before statewide vital records (1916). Major denominations: Methodist (strongest in downstate IL), Baptist (southern IL), Presbyterian, Congregational (northern IL), Catholic (German, Irish, Polish, Italian, Lithuanian parishes), Lutheran (German and Scandinavian), Amish/Mennonite (Douglas, Woodford counties), and numerous others. French Catholic parishes in the American Bottom date to 1699. Many records microfilmed by FamilySearch.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Illinois' 102 counties. Illinois has thousands of small rural and church cemeteries in addition to municipal cemeteries. Many Illinois genealogical societies have published cemetery transcription volumes. Illinois State Genealogical Society coordinates statewide cemetery projects.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society headquartered in Springfield. Publishes the Illinois State Genealogical Society Quarterly. Maintains databases, surname files, and coordinates with county-level societies across all 102 counties. Annual conferences and webinars.

Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, obituary lookups, historical sketches, Bible records, county histories, and county-specific research guides for all 102 Illinois counties.
Illinois Secretary of State website provides access to Illinois State Archives databases including land tract sales, military records, and other searchable databases useful for genealogical research.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Illinois has no abolished or extinct counties currently. All 102 counties exist today. However, county boundaries changed significantly as new counties were carved from existing ones throughout the 19th century. The original Illinois counties under the Northwest Territory were St. Clair (1790) and Randolph (1795). From these (and later creations), all 102 modern counties were eventually formed. The last county formed was Ford County (1859) and Douglas County (1859). Researchers should carefully trace parent county chains since records before a county's formation year are in the parent county. Additionally, some early territory-era records may be found in Indiana Territory records (1800-1809) or Northwest Territory records.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Cook County is the only major 'burned county' in Illinois due to the 1871 Great Chicago Fire, which destroyed the Cook County courthouse and most pre-1871 county records including deeds, court files, probate records, and vital records. Some records were reconstructed from private copies and duplicate filings. Other Illinois counties have had minor fire or flood damage, but none resulted in catastrophic county-wide record loss comparable to Cook County.
